coredata.dc:title: Multi-color carbon dots from cis-butenedioic acid and urea and highly luminescent carbon dots@Ca(OH)2 hybrid phosphors with excellent thermal stability for white light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Bingjie
Liu, Yunpeng
Cao, Mengmeng
Zhu, Mengmeng
Chen, Renjie
Li, Huili
coredata.dc:description: Multi-color carbon dots (CDs) were obtained from cis-butenedioic acid (C-BA) and urea by a simple one-step solvothermal route. This is the first report about using C-BA as the raw material to synthesize CDs. The fluorescent properties of as-prepared CDs vary with the ratio of C-BA to urea. And the sample with 1:1 of C-BA and urea exhibits the optimal yellow emission and highest photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 16.17%. Further investigations indicate that the variation of PL properties comes from the different content of surface functional groups such as CC, CO, –COOH, and graphitic N. Both of CO and graphitic N cause the PL redshift, whereas the CC and –COOH groups lead to the blueshift. Based on the above, a solid-state luminescent CDs-based phosphor was obtained by adding alkaline Ca(OH)2 powder to as-prepared yellow CDs solution. In this case, PL intensity is increased to 1.78 times, the corresponding emission redshifts from 553 to 568 nm, and PLQY is improved from 16.17% to 35.12%. Simultaneously, CDs@Ca(OH)2 hybrid phosphor exhibits good thermostability. Finally, a white light-emitting diode (LED) was successfully fabricated by combining the yellow-emission CDs-based phosphor with a blue LED chip. It exhibits a high CRI of 86.9, the CCT of 5388 K, and a luminous efficacy of 15.12 lm/W. The CIE coordinate locates at (0.3341, 0.3075) which is very close to the pure white light of (0.33, 0.33). Thus, as-prepared CDs-based solid-state phosphor is a potential candidate for practical lighting devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cis-butenedioic acid (C-BA)
Solid-state phosphors
Ca(OH)2
White LEDs

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of CDs phosphors-based white LEDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shanghai Municipality Science and Technology Commission

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process for multi-color CDs by solvothermal and the corresponding CDs photographs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right), respectively.
TEM (a1-a5) and HRTEM (b1-b5) images of five different CDs samples prepared with precursor ratio of 1:4, 2:3, 1:1, 3:2, and 4:1, respectively.
Emission spectra of five different CDs samples (a–e).
The original (a) and normalized (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the five CDs. Inset in (a) is the local magnification at 340–380 nm.
FTIR spectra of five different CDs samples.
Magnification and fitting curves of the C 1s peak for five different CDs samples.
Magnification and fitting curves of the N 1s peak for five different CDs samples.
The possible growth mechanism for CDs.
The emission spectra of CDs-3 and CDs-based SSP under 480 nm excitation.
UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-3, CDs-6, and solid Ca(OH)2.
The possible reaction mechanism between CDs and Ca(OH)2.
TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of CDs-6.
The normalized fluorescent intensities of CDs-6, CDs-7, and commercial YAG:Ce phosphors heated to various temperatures in air atmosphere.
The EL spectra (a, inset is the photograph under forward current driving) and CIE coordinates (b) of white LED fabricated by using CDs-6 as a phosphor converter.
XPS analyses of the C1s spectra for five CDs samples.
XPS analyses of the N1s spectra for five CDs samples.
The comparison of properties for white LEDs based on CDs phosphors.

head.ce:title: Multi-color carbon dots from cis-butenedioic acid and urea and highly luminescent carbon dots@Ca(OH)2 hybrid phosphors with excellent thermal stability for white light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics and Advanced Instrument, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Electronic Science, East China Normal University, Shanghai, 200241, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics and Advanced Instrument
School of Physics and Electronic Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics and Advanced Instrument, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Electronic Science, East China Normal University, Shanghai, 200241, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. East China Normal University, Shanghai, 200241, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-color carbon dots (CDs) were obtained from cis-butenedioic acid (C-BA) and urea by a simple one-step solvothermal route. This is the first report about using C-BA as the raw material to synthesize CDs. The fluorescent properties of as-prepared CDs vary with the ratio of C-BA to urea. And the sample with 1:1 of C-BA and urea exhibits the optimal yellow emission and highest photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 16.17%. Further investigations indicate that the variation of PL properties comes from the different content of surface functional groups such as CC, CO, –COOH, and graphitic N. Both of CO and graphitic N cause the PL redshift, whereas the CC and –COOH groups lead to the blueshift. Based on the above, a solid-state luminescent CDs-based phosphor was obtained by adding alkaline Ca(OH)2 powder to as-prepared yellow CDs solution. In this case, PL intensity is increased to 1.78 times, the corresponding emission redshifts from 553 to 568 nm, and PLQY is improved from 16.17% to 35.12%. Simultaneously, CDs@Ca(OH)2 hybrid phosphor exhibits good thermostability. Finally, a white light-emitting diode (LED) was successfully fabricated by combining the yellow-emission CDs-based phosphor with a blue LED chip. It exhibits a high CRI of 86.9, the CCT of 5388 K, and a luminous efficacy of 15.12 lm/W. The CIE coordinate locates at (0.3341, 0.3075) which is very close to the pure white light of (0.33, 0.33). Thus, as-prepared CDs-based solid-state phosphor is a potential candidate for practical lighting devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as one of the members of carbon nanomaterials, first reported in 2004 [1], have attracted more and more scientists and scholars' attentions due to their adjustable and excellent optical properties [2], considerable photostability, low toxicity and cost-effectiveness [3], friendly environment [4,5], super biocompatibility and water solubility [6,7], simple preparation, as well as easy surface modification. Therefore, they have been applied in various fields in recent years, such as light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [8], photocatalysis [9], drug delivery [10], sensors [11], fluorescent anti-counterfeiting [12,13], photovoltaic devices, cancer treatment [14], and biomedicines [15].

According to the reports, CDs are composed of carbon nuclei with sp2 and sp3 hybridised carbon atoms and abundant surface groups, typically as –COOH, –OH, –CO, and –NH2 functional groups [16]. These surface moieties possess different energy levels and can be modified or altered by external environment and post-treatment, which leads to numerous electronic transition possibilities [17] and various luminescent properties [18,19]. Therefore, PL properties of CDs are considered to be mainly governed by surface states [20]. Yang et al. obtained full-color CDs from citric acid (CA) and urea by controlling reaction temperature, and revealed that the synthesizing temperature had a huge impact on surface states of CDs [21]. Liu and coworkers prepared CDs with tunable surface states via a hydrothermal method using p-phenylenediamine and different raw materials containing amino groups as carbon and nitrogen sources. It was disclosed that the fluorescence of CDs mainly originated from the surface states related to nitrogen speciation [22]. Ma et al. synthesized multicolor fluorescent CDs by changing the solvent type and the precursor ratio of citric acid to urea. The results indicated that the difference of the fluorescence properties among four kinds of CDs arose from the different surface states and contents of pyridinic N [23]. These researches further demonstrated that surface state tailoring of CDs has been regarded as an efficient way to modulate the PL emission of CDs. It can be realized by surface functionalization and passivation, postoxidation, increasing oxygen/nitrogen contents through choosing appropriate raw materials or/and controlling the reaction conditions [24]. Each CA molecule contains three –COOH groups with the mass ratio of 70.26%. Therefore, it has been widely reported that CA is an excellent precursor to prepare CDs because a large number of carboxyl functional groups can not only enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs but also make emission redshift by increasing content of –COOH groups on the CDs surface [25]. Similar to CA, cis-butenedioic acid (C-BA) also contains a large number of carboxyl groups. Each molecule of C-BA contains two –COOH groups with the mass ratio of 77.59% which is higher than CA and one –CC- conjugated structure which is expected to enhance PL intensity and cause emission redshift. Thus, C-BA should be an ideal starting material to synthesize CDs with high PL intensity and long-wavelength emission. To our best knowledge, no related works are reported until now.

Recently, CDs-based solid-state phosphors (SSP) have received growing attention as a potential alternative of conventional rare-earth-based phosphors for lighting and displays because of their low cost, friendly environment, adjustable optical properties, and very broad emission wavelength. However, for most solid-state CDs, PL is usually greatly reduced and even disappears due to the aggregation-induced luminescent quenching (AILQ) effect, which seriously hinders their applications. The common process of avoiding AILQ is to incorporate CDs into solid-state matrices, like silica [26], polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [27], starch [28], inorganic salts [29], and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [30], by adsorption, physical embedding, chemical bonding, as well as surface engineering [28,29,31–33]. Additionally, solid-state CDs were also directly synthesized by modifying the precursor surface in some reported works [34,35]. However, it is still unsatisfactory that these reported solid-state CDs exhibited either decreasing PL quantum yields (PLQYs) or inferior thermal stability because thermal stability of solid-state matrices themselves were bad. As we know that in practical applications, an excellent luminescent device requires not only high PLQYs of the phosphors but also a good thermal stability. Hence, a combination of the high PLQYs and the good thermal stability in CDs-based SSP for white LEDs is strongly desirable. That is to say, choosing an appropriate solid-state matrix is very important and pivotal. It has been reported that alkali can play a pivotal role on the surface electron state of CDs [36]. Its introduction can decrease the band gap and cause the red shift of CDs fluorescent emission [36]. Ca(OH)2 belongs to the strong base with high pH value. It has good thermal stability in comparison with PVA, starch, and MOFs and low solubility in water about 1.2 g/L (25 °C). The strong alkalinity of Ca(OH)2 can provide advantageous condition for the lower-energy emission of CDs with enhanced thermal stability and water resistance [37,38]. As far as we know, there are no related reports on the preparation of CDs-based SSP using Ca(OH)2 as matrix material. All the stated above are what have been the focuses of the present study.

In this work, we obtained five multi-color CDs from different ratio of C-BA/urea with 1:4, 2:3, 1:1, 3:2, and 4:1 by a simple one-step solvothermal route, corresponding to CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, CDs-4, CDs-5, respectively. The C-BA has a great contribution to enhance photoluminescence of CDs and urea plays an important role for the redshift of emission wavelength. This is the first report about using C-BA as the raw material to synthesize CDs. Each molecule of C-BA contains two –COOH groups with the mass ratio of 77.59% higher than CA, and has a conjugated structure of CC. Interestingly, the fluorescence colors of these five CDs solutions vary from blue-green (CDs-1, CDs-2) to yellow (CDs-3), and back to blue-green (CDs-4, CDs-5) again. While CDs prepared by urea and C-BA alone exhibit blue emission. The detailed investigations show that the five CDs have similar particle size and the same types of functional groups, but the content of different functional groups varies greatly. Under the excitation of optimal wavelength of 460 nm, CDs-3 give a broad emission band from 500 to 650 nm peaked at 553 nm. Based on these, a yellow solid-state CDs-based phosphor (CDs-6) was obtained by adding Ca(OH)2 powder to CDs-3 solution. The corresponding emission redshifts from 553 to 568 nm and PLQY enhances from 16.17% to 35.12%. Finally, a white LED was successfully fabricated by combining the CDs-6 phosphor with the blue LED chip of emission peak at 460 nm. The Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) color coordinate locates at (0.3341, 0.3075), color rendering index (CRI) is 86.9, and the correlated color temperature (CCT) is 5388 K.

C-BA, urea, ethanol, and calcium hydroxide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dialysis bag and dialysis membrane were purchased from To-science Co., Ltd. The blue LED chips were obtained from Shenzhen Photoelectric Co., Ltd. The transparent silicone (YD65-5A, YD65-5B) used for white LED packaging was purchased from Materials Science and Technology (Shenzhen) Co., Ltd.

Firstly, the total amount of C-BA and urea was fixed at 0.005 mol. Then, different molar ratio of C-BA/urea with 1:4, 2:3, 1:1, 3:2, and 4:1 was separately added to 40 mL ethanol (GR) and stirred strongly until they were completely dissolved. The resultant transparent solutions were transferred into 50 mL poly (para-phenol)-lined stainless-steel autoclaves and heated to 200 °C for 10 h to react in an oven. After cooling down to room temperature (RT) naturally, the solutions were dialyzed separately using the dialysis membranes with the molecular weight of 500 Da for 48 h in distilled water and further purified by filtering with a diameter of 0.22 μm organic filter membrane. Finally, five kinds of CDs solutions were obtained, named as CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, CDs-4, CDs-5, respectively.

0.10, 0.20, 0.40, 0.60 and 1.0 g Ca(OH)2 powder were added to 10 mL as-prepared CDs-3 solution, and stirred strongly with a magnetic stirring apparatus at RT for 5 h. The resultant mixing solution was centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and then the precipitate was dried at 50 °C for 2 h under vacuum to obtain CDs-based SSP. The sample with 0.20 g Ca(OH)2 was called as CDs-6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs phosphors-based white LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-6 was mixed with the transparent silicone (YD65-5A) and anhydride curing agent (YD65-5B) (YD65-5A: YD65-5B = 1:1 in weight). The bubbles were removed by grounding for 10 min by hand. Then, the resultant mixture slurry was added to a blue-chip (λpeak = 460 nm) carefully by dipping and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 4 h to form a yellow-emissive CDs-based phosphor film. Finally, a white LED based on a single-color CDs phosphor converter was successfully fabricated and further characterized.

The ultraviolet/visible (UV/Vis) absorption spectra of the CDs were measured by using a spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Lambda 950). PL spectra and PLQYs were acquired by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, FluoroMax-4) containing an integrating sphere unit (Horiba Jobin Yvon, F3029). The morphology was observed by a JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL, Japan). The chemical environments, element compositions, and functional groups of the samples were analyzed by a multifunction imaging photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) and a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet Instrument Co., Nicolet Nexus 600, USA). The optical properties such as emission spectra, luminous efficiency, CRI, CCT, and CIE color coordinates of as-fabricated white LEDs were investigated via a NUV/Vis/Near IR spectrophoto-colorimeter (PMS-80).

The multi-color fluorescent CDs were synthesized by a facile solvothermal reaction, as shown in Fig. 1 . Urea and C-BA were chosen as precursors for the first time. Similar to CA, C-BA as carbon source can provide a large amount of –COOH groups and the additional conjugated structure of CC, which is expected to enhance PL intensity. And urea supplies N elements and the related functional groups for as-prepared CDs. PL properties of CDs can be easily tailored by simply controlling the molar ratio of urea to C-BA. The fluorescent colors of the five CDs solutions vary from blue-green, yellow-green, to yellow, and back to blue-green under UV light.

Fig. 2 shows TEM and HRTEM images of the five CDs samples with precursor mole ratio of 1:4, 2:3, 1:1, 3:2, and 4:1, respectively. All the CDs samples exhibit good solubility, dispersity and homogeneity in the ethanol with similar average particle size of 1.86, 1.33, 1.25, 1.57, and 1.35 nm. HRTEM images (b1-b5) show well-resolved lattice fringes of 0.21 nm apart corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon, indicating that the particles own highly crystalline carbon structure [39]. Similar TEM morphology and size reveal that multi-color emissions of five CDs should not be attributed to the traditional quantum size effect.

Fig. 3 gives the emission spectra of CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, CDs-4 and CDs-5 under different excitation wavelengths. All five CDs exhibit excitation-dependent emissive features. The fluorescence emissions gradually redshift in response to increasing the excitation wavelength. The optimal excitation wavelengths of five CDs are 370, 400, 480, 430, and 390 nm with the corresponding emission peaks at 443, 473, 553, 526, and 453 nm in sequence. However, CDs prepared at the same heating condition (200 °C for 10 h) by using single C-BA or urea as the precursor show almost excitation-independent emission (Fig. S1). Their emission peaks under the optimal excitation wavelength both locate at 430 nm. Thus, we can speculate that some chemical reactions between urea and C-BA maybe take place during heating which contributes to the redshift of the emission spectrum. This interesting phenomenon need to be further confirmed by more characterizations.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the five CDs solutions were also measured and shown in Fig. 4 . Obviously, the absorption characteristics of five CDs are very similar. All contain two clear absorption peaks at 203–209 nm and 266–275 nm, which are respectively assigned to π–π* transitions from the aromatic sp2 domains (CC) and n–π* transitions from defect states of CO bonds according to previous reports [40,41]. Besides, a weak absorption band peaked at 340–380 nm (the inset in Fig. 4a) is also observed which should originate from energy level transitions caused by surface groups [8]. These phenomena suggest that five samples possess the same luminous centers but different surface conditions. Furthermore, CDs-3 exhibits the lowest absorption at 203–209 nm while the maximum at 266–275 nm. Thus, it can be concluded that the different proportion of C-BA to urea will lead to the change of functional group contents on as-prepared CDs surface. For CDs-3, the lowest absorption at 203–209 nm means that it contains the minimum content of CC, and the maximum at 266–275 nm indicates that it owns the highest CO groups among the five samples.

To investigate the detailed chemical bonds and functional groups on these CDs samples, FTIR characterizations were done and shown in Fig. 5 . Five CDs also show extremely similar FTIR spectra, which further reveals the existence of similar chemical compositions. The broad peak emerging at 3433 cm−1 comes from stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H [23]. The peaks located at 2985 cm−1 and 2942 cm−1 are the vibrations of = C–H and -C-H [42]. The peak located at 1720 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of CO from carboxy [23]. The absorption peaks at 1031 cm−1 and 786 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of C–OH [43]. The peak located at 1580 cm−1 is stretching vibrations of CO (carbonyl) [42]. Besides these, other functional groups can also be observed, including C–N (1400 cm−1) and C–OH (1190 cm−1, carboxy) [18,23]. In conclusion, there are a lot of hydroxy, amino, amide, carboxy and carbonyl functional groups on the as-prepared CDs and N element is doped successfully.

To know more details about the surface chemistry, CDs were further analyzed by XPS and the results are presented in Fig. S2. All samples show three typical peaks corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, respectively. The high-resolution C1s peaks were analyzed and fitted in Fig. 6 a–e and Table 1 . Obviously, all C1s spectra can be deconvoluted into four peaks being attributed to sp2 carbon (CC/C–C), sp3 carbon (C–O/C–N), carbonyl carbon (CO), and carboxyl carbon (COOH). Their different integrated intensities reveal that CDs-3 contains the most abundant CO groups, while the least COOH and CC groups. This maybe be attributed to the dehydration condensation between urea and C-BA because of 1:1 stoichiometric ratio [44]. As a result, a large number of CO groups are produced, whose n–π* transitions lead to a redshift in the spectrum and yellow emission. Instead, π–π* transitions from –COOH and CC groups cause a blueshift in the spectrum. In addition, the N1s spectra are shown in Fig. 7 a–e. The N 1s spectra can be fitted well with three curves corresponding to amino, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively. By analysis and calculation, it can be found the highest content of graphitic N in CDs-3 (see Table 2 ). This is another reason causing long wavelength fluorescent emission. We also speculate that addition reactions may exist in CC bonds. One of the C–C single bonds breaks and nitrogen atom is added to form graphitic N, which introduces a new energy level and results in a redshift in the spectra. In the O1s spectra (Figs. S3a–e), the three optimized peaks correspond to the OC, O–C, and COO. The total content variation of CO, O–C, and COO is consistent with the C 1s spectrum analysis (Table S1). The above XPS analyses match well with the results of UV–vis absorption and FTIR spectra in Figs. 4 and 5.

Based on the above discussion, it can be concluded that the different PL properties of as-prepared CDs should be mainly ascribed to the diverse surface states, especially graphite N, carbonyl, carboxyl and CC groups contents. The diverse surface states result from different levels of dehydration condensation and addition reactions between urea and C-BA in the process of forming CDs because of the various ratio. The related reaction mechanism is speculated and shown in Fig. 8 . When the molar ratio of urea to C-BA is 1:1, –NH2 groups and –COOH groups will completely dehydrate and condense to form CO groups. And at the same time, the addition reactions between CC and the N in –NH– groups further form a larger conjugated structure. The N atoms completely connect with C atoms to form graphite N, which increases the rigidity of the CDs molecule and improves its stability. Similar reaction mechanism had been reported by Yang and co-workers [42]. Therefore, in this work, it can be concluded that CC and –COOH groups on CDs cause a blueshift, while CO and graphitic N lead to a redshift of emission spectrum. And as a result, CDs-3 owns the long-wavelength emission and presents a bright yellow fluorescence among all as-prepared CDs.

In order to verify the luminescent stability of CDs, we measured the PL variation of CDs-3 at different storage time and UV irradiation time, as shown in Figs.S4 and S5. The sample remains at a same place for six months under RT. Surprisingly, almost no any decrease in PL intensity can be observed in CDs-3, indicating that the synthesized CDs in our work own the excellent luminescent stability. It needs to be noted that the optimal excitation wavelength of the CDs-3 locates at 480 nm with a typical yellow emission peaked at 553 nm. If the CDs-3 based solid-state phosphor can be fabricated, moreover, the excellent PL properties are remained, it should be an ideal luminescent material for white LED application.

As we know that the strong alkalinity of Ca(OH)2 can provide advantageous condition for the lower-energy emission of CDs with enhanced thermal stability and water resistance [37,38]. Some reports have confirmed that alkali can promote the formation of quinone structure of CDs, resulting into an electron-rich property. The rich electrons can lift the HOMO to a higher energy level, which causes the redshift of absorption and emission [36,45]. Moreover, there are no related reports on the preparation of CDs-based SSP using Ca(OH)2 as a matrix material till now. Thus, in our present work, different weights of calcium hydroxide were added to 10 mL CDs-3 solution to obtain CDs-based SSP. As shown in Fig. 9 , all the CDs-based SSP give a redshift emission peaked at 568 nm under 480 nm excitation in comparison with 553 nm of CDs-3. Simultaneously, the enhancement of fluorescent intensity is always accompanied and finally the SSP with 0.2 g Ca(OH)2, named as CDs-6, exhibits the highest intensity, which is 1.78 times of CDs-3. The corresponding PLQY is improved from 16.17% to 35.12%. Fig. 10 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-3, CDs-6, and pure Ca(OH)2 powder. Surprisingly, a strong broad absorption band in the visible light region of 400–650 nm appears in CDs-6, which may be the reason of emission red-shift and PLQY enhancement. And this speculation needs to be further proven in the following FTIR analysis.

Figs. S6 gives FTIR spectra of CDs-3, CDs-6, and pure Ca(OH)2 material. By comparison, it can be found that the peak located at 1720 cm−1 from the stretching vibration of CO (carboxy) disappears but a new absorption peak at 1340 cm−1 appears for CDs-6. It has been reported that even though there exist CO groups in both carboxylic acid and carboxylate, the positions of their absorption peaks are different in FTIR. Absorption peak locates around 1721 cm−1 for carboxylic acid, while absorption peak for carboxylate appears at 1384 cm−1 [46]. Additionally, the peak at 1687 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration band of CO in the quinone structure [36]. Therefore, it can be speculated that Ca(OH)2 reacts with –COOH on the surface of CDs to produce calcium carboxylate when Ca(OH)2 mixes with CDs. This deprotonation process leads to the appearance of more electron-rich surface groups. And simultaneously, the strong alkali promotes the formation of quinone structure with rich electrons in CDs.

According to the literatures, phenolphthalein with the quinone structure under alkaline conditions usually exhibit an absorption band at 550 nm [36]. Moreover, deprotonation process of CDs at basic condition also results in a lower vibrational energy dissipation [47]. Combining absorption spectra in Fig. 10 with FTIR in Fig. S6, it can be thought that the new absorption peak at 400–650 nm of CDs-6 in Fig. 10 should come from the increased electron-rich surface groups such as CO groups of quinone structure. The rich electrons of surface groups lift the HOMO to higher energy levels, which causes the redshifts of absorption and emission. The possible reaction process of CDs and Ca(OH)2 shows in Fig. 11 .

Fig. 12 shows TEM and HRTEM images of CDs-6. Clearly, spherical nano-CDs distribute homogeneously on the surface of large-size Ca(OH)2 particles. Distinct lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.208 nm and 0.26 nm correspond to (100) plane of graphitic carbon and (011) plane of Ca(OH)2, respectively. More importantly, no obvious aggregates of CDs are present. All the above prove again that there must exist some chemical bonding between CDs and Ca(OH)2 not only simple physical adsorption. For most QDs materials, PL intensity is usually greatly reduced and even disappears due to the AILQ effect in solid state. In our work, effective dispersion of small CDs on the surface of large Ca(OH)2 particles by chemical bonding can lengthen the distance among CDs. And as a result, AILQ is steadily hindered. The corresponding PL intensity and PLQY are improved dramatically. These results are very beneficial to white LED application.

As we know that the working temperature of LEDs can reach 130 °C, so it is required for LED phosphors to possess good thermostability. Fig. 13 gives the normalized integrated intensity of CDs-6 heated in the air from 30 °C to 240 °C. In order to compare, phosphor prepared by using CDs-3 combined with 0.1 g organic starch matrix, named as CDs-7, and commercial YAG:Ce were also measured and the results present in the same figure. Even though PL intensities of three samples gradually decrease with temperature increasing, speeds of decline are different and increase in sequence: YAG:Ce < CDs-6 < CDs-7. At 150 °C, PL intensity of CDs@Ca(OH)2 under 480 nm excitation still maintains 53.5% of the initial intensity, which is much better than CDs@starch phosphor whose emission intensity at 150 °C decreases to 31.6% of that at RT, and slightly worse than 77.3% of commercial YAG:Ce phosphor. The relatively good thermostability of CDs@Ca(OH)2 can meet the application requirements of white LED. Additionally, as we know that it is difficult for YAG:Ce-based white LEDs to achieve a high color rendering index (Ra) due to the lack of red components in the YAG:Ce emission. However, CDs based SSP can well solve this problem due to their very broad emission range, which will be proven in Fig. 14 . Thus, this kind of CDs based SSP should have a great potential and could be a good substitute for traditional yellow YAG:Ce phosphors in white LED application.

Fig. 14 gives the characteristics of a white LED based on as-prepared CDs-6 phosphor combining with a blue LED chip. Under operation of 20 mA, it exhibits a broad emission spectrum ranging from 420 nm to 750 nm, almost covering the entire visible area. The emission peaked at 584 nm comes from CDs-6 SSP and the peak located in 460 nm belongs to the blue LED chip. Even though a single CDs-6 yellow phosphor layer is adopted, the fabricated white LED owns a higher CRI of 86.9 than those reported in other related works (Table 3 ). The CCT and luminous efficacy are 5388 K and 15.12 lm/W, respectively. The CIE coordinate locates at (0.3341, 0.3075) which is very close to the pure white light of (0.33, 0.33). Of course, the EL properties of white LED can be regulated by varying the CDs content and characteristics in Ca(OH)2/CDs phosphors to meet some special requirements. In short, as-prepared CDs-based solid-state phosphors are potential candidates for practical lighting applications.

In summary, we successfully synthesized multi-color CDs by controlling the ratio of C-BA and urea for the first time. As-prepared CDs own good solubility, dispersity and homogeneous morphology but different optical properties. The emission of as-prepared CDs redshifts gradually from blue-green to yellow with C-BA content increasing, and reaches the maximum at 1:1 of C-BA and urea. The detailed investigations show that the variation of PL properties comes from the different content surface functional groups such as CC, CO, –COOH and graphitic N. Both of CO and graphitic N cause the PL redshift, whereas the CC and –COOH group lead to the blueshift. Finally, a yellow solid-state CDs-based phosphor was obtained by adding Ca(OH)2 powder to as-prepared yellow CDs solution. The corresponding emission redshifts from 553 to 568 nm and PLQY enhances from 16.17% to 35.12%. The yellow solid-state CDs-based phosphor with good thermostability was applied to white LED successfully. The CRI, CCT, CIE color coordinate and luminous efficacy are 86.9, 5388 K, (0.3341, 0.3075) and 15.12 lm/W, respectively.

Bingjie Yu:the main task preparing CDs materials, test and writing; Yunpeng Liu: Assist preparing CDs, PL, PLQY test; Mengmeng Cao: Assist WLED test; Mengmeng Zhu, Renjie Chen: Assist PL test; Huili Li: Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Shanghai Municipal Natural Science Foundation (No. 19ZR1415400) and Joint Institute of Advanced Science and Technology, East China Normal University, Shanghai 200062, People's Republic of China (No. 40500-20105-222053).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2021.118202.
